audit_status.review_note: Reviewed verse by verse for major Hebrew wording, grammar, context, and interpretive translation choices. Final scholarly/source-critical review may still refine details.

verses.restored: And the human knew Eve his woman, and she conceived and bore Cain. And she said, I have acquired a man with the Eternal.

familiar.text: And Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain, and said, I have gotten a man from the LORD.

notes.title: Knew
notes.body: Yada, knew, is used here as intimate sexual knowing. The text links embodiment, conception, and birth without shame language.

notes.title: Cain wordplay
notes.body: Cain is linked with qanah, to acquire or get. Eve interprets birth as receiving/acquiring with the Eternal.

verses.restored: And again she bore his brother Abel. Abel became a keeper of flocks, and Cain became a servant of the ground.

familiar.text: And she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground.

notes.title: Abel
notes.body: Abel, Hevel, also means vapor or breath, a name that becomes haunting in a chapter about fragile life.

notes.title: Servant of the ground
notes.body: Cain works the adamah, the ground connected with human origin and post-Eden toil.

verses.restored: And at the end of days, Cain brought from the fruit of the ground an offering to the Eternal.

familiar.text: And in process of time it came to pass, that Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto the LORD.

notes.title: End of days
notes.body: The phrase marks the passing of time. Cain brings an offering from the ground he serves.

verses.restored: And Abel also brought from the firstborn of his flock and from their fat portions. And the Eternal looked with regard toward Abel and his offering.

familiar.text: And Abel, he also brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof. And the LORD had respect unto Abel and to his offering:

notes.title: Firstborn and fat portions
notes.body: Abel’s offering is described with language of first and rich portions. The text gives more detail to Abel’s offering than Cain’s.

notes.title: Looked with regard
notes.body: Shaah means to look toward or regard. The text does not fully explain why Abel is regarded.

verses.restored: But toward Cain and his offering, the Eternal did not look with regard. Cain burned greatly with anger, and his face fell.

familiar.text: But unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect. And Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell.

notes.title: Anger and fallen face
notes.body: Cain’s inner state becomes visible in his face. The chapter focuses less on ritual mechanics and more on the response to rejection.

verses.restored: And the Eternal said to Cain, Why have you burned with anger, and why has your face fallen?

familiar.text: And the LORD said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen?

notes.title: Divine question
notes.body: As in Genesis 3, the Divine question draws hidden inner reality into awareness.

verses.restored: If you do well, will there not be lifting up? And if you do not do well, sin is crouching at the doorway. Its longing is toward you, but you must rule over it.

familiar.text: If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted? and if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him.

notes.title: Sin crouching
notes.body: Chattat, sin or offense, is pictured almost like a crouching animal at the doorway. This is not merely guilt; it is a force or pattern seeking entry.

notes.title: Longing and rule
notes.body: Teshuqah and mashal echo Genesis 3:16. Here the human is told that the destructive impulse desires him, but he must rule over it.

notes.title: Doorway
notes.body: The doorway image makes this one of the clearest early texts about inner accountability before violence is enacted.

verses.restored: And Cain spoke to Abel his brother. And when they were in the field, Cain rose against Abel his brother and killed him.

familiar.text: And Cain talked with Abel his brother: and it came to pass, when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him.

notes.title: Textual gap
notes.body: The Masoretic text says Cain spoke to Abel but does not include what he said. Some ancient witnesses add, Let us go out to the field. The Masoretic reading leaves an ominous silence.

notes.title: Brother repeated
notes.body: Brother is repeated, intensifying the horror: the first murder is fratricide.

verses.restored: And the Eternal said to Cain, Where is Abel your brother? And he said, I do not know. Am I my brother’s keeper?

familiar.text: And the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother? And he said, I know not: Am I my brother's keeper?

notes.title: Where is your brother?
notes.body: The question echoes Where are you? from Genesis 3, but now accountability includes the other.

notes.title: Keeper
notes.body: Shamar means keep, guard, watch, or preserve. Cain denies responsibility for the brother he has destroyed.

verses.restored: And the Divine said, What have you done? The voice of your brother’s blood is crying to me from the ground.

familiar.text: And he said, What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground.

notes.title: Blood crying
notes.body: The blood has a voice. Violence is not silent in creation; the ground itself becomes witness.

notes.title: Blood plural
notes.body: The Hebrew can be read as bloods, often understood as bloodshed or the fullness of life taken.

verses.restored: And now, cursed are you from the ground, which opened its mouth to receive your brother’s blood from your hand.

familiar.text: And now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand;

notes.title: Cursed from the ground
notes.body: Cain is cursed from the ground. The ground that received Abel’s blood now becomes the site of Cain’s alienation.

verses.restored: When you serve the ground, it will no longer give its strength to you. You shall be a fugitive and a wanderer on the earth.

familiar.text: When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength; a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth.

notes.title: Ground withholding strength
notes.body: The rupture between human and ground deepens. Cain’s vocation as servant of the ground is broken by bloodshed.

notes.title: Fugitive and wanderer
notes.body: The doubled language marks displacement, instability, and exile.

verses.restored: And Cain said to the Eternal, My punishment is greater than I can bear.

familiar.text: And Cain said unto the LORD, My punishment is greater than I can bear.

notes.title: Punishment or iniquity
notes.body: Avon can mean iniquity, guilt, or punishment. Cain’s words may carry both the burden of guilt and the consequence of it.

verses.restored: Look, you have driven me today from the face of the ground, and from your face I will be hidden. I shall be a fugitive and a wanderer on the earth, and whoever finds me will kill me.

familiar.text: Behold, thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the earth; and from thy face shall I be hid; and I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth; and it shall come to pass, that every one that findeth me shall slay me.

notes.title: Face of ground and face of God
notes.body: Cain names exile from both ground and divine face. Violence has ruptured vertical and earthly belonging.

verses.restored: And the Eternal said to him, Therefore, whoever kills Cain, vengeance shall be taken sevenfold. And the Eternal set a sign for Cain, so that no one finding him would strike him down.

familiar.text: And the LORD said unto him, Therefore whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold. And the LORD set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him.

notes.title: Sign for Cain
notes.body: The mark/sign is protective, not a racial marker and not a curse itself. It restrains revenge after the first murder.

notes.title: Sevenfold vengeance
notes.body: The text limits retaliation by placing Cain under divine protection despite his guilt.

verses.restored: And Cain went out from the face of the Eternal and settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden.

familiar.text: And Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden.

notes.title: East of Eden
notes.body: Cain moves farther east, extending the exile movement from Eden.

notes.title: Nod
notes.body: Nod is linked with wandering, echoing Cain’s sentence as wanderer.

verses.restored: And Cain knew his woman, and she conceived and bore Enoch. And he was building a city, and he called the name of the city after the name of his son, Enoch.

familiar.text: And Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch: and he builded a city, and called the name of the city, after the name of his son, Enoch.

notes.title: City after exile
notes.body: Cain’s line begins city-building after wandering. The text opens the question of culture, protection, and permanence outside Eden.

verses.restored: To Enoch was born Irad. Irad fathered Mehujael, Mehujael fathered Methushael, and Methushael fathered Lamech.

familiar.text: And unto Enoch was born Irad: and Irad begat Mehujael: and Mehujael begat Methusael: and Methusael begat Lamech.

notes.title: Lineage
notes.body: The genealogy traces Cain’s line toward Lamech, where violence and culture intensify.

verses.restored: And Lamech took for himself two women. The name of the one was Adah, and the name of the second was Zillah.

familiar.text: And Lamech took unto him two wives: the name of the one was Adah, and the name of the other Zillah.

notes.title: Two women
notes.body: This is the first explicit polygamy notice in Genesis, attached to Cain’s line.

verses.restored: And Adah bore Jabal. He was the father of those who dwell in tents and have livestock.

familiar.text: And Adah bare Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and of such as have cattle.

notes.title: Culture begins
notes.body: Cain’s line is associated with the development of social and economic life: tents and livestock.

verses.restored: His brother’s name was Jubal. He was the father of all who handle lyre and pipe.

familiar.text: And his brother's name was Jubal: he was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ.

notes.title: Music
notes.body: The line of Cain also produces music. The text does not portray culture as simply evil, even when violence is present in the line.

verses.restored: And Zillah also bore Tubal-Cain, forger of every tool of bronze and iron. And the sister of Tubal-Cain was Naamah.

familiar.text: And Zillah, she also bare Tubalcain, an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron: and the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah.

notes.title: Bronze and iron
notes.body: Technology appears in Cain’s line alongside music and livestock. The chapter places human culture in an ambiguous moral field.

verses.restored: And Lamech said to his women, Adah and Zillah, hear my voice; women of Lamech, listen to my speech. For I have killed a man for wounding me, a young man for striking me.

familiar.text: And Lamech said unto his wives, Adah and Zillah, Hear my voice; ye wives of Lamech, hearken unto my speech: for I have slain a man to my wounding, and a young man to my hurt.

notes.title: Song of violence
notes.body: Lamech’s speech is poetic and boastful. Violence has moved from Cain’s shame into Lamech’s self-display.

verses.restored: If Cain is avenged sevenfold, then Lamech seventy-sevenfold.

familiar.text: If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold.

notes.title: Escalated vengeance
notes.body: Cain’s protection from revenge becomes, in Lamech’s mouth, a boast of multiplied vengeance. Violence escalates through the line.

verses.restored: And Adam knew his woman again, and she bore a son and called his name Seth, for she said, The Divine has appointed for me another seed instead of Abel, because Cain killed him.

familiar.text: And Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called his name Seth: For God, said she, hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew.

notes.title: Seth
notes.body: Seth is linked with appointing or placing. His birth is framed as another seed after Abel’s death.

verses.restored: And to Seth also a son was born, and he called his name Enosh. Then people began to call on the name of the Eternal.

familiar.text: And to Seth, to him also there was born a son; and he called his name Enos: then began men to call upon the name of the LORD.

notes.title: Enosh
notes.body: Enosh means human, mortal, or humankind, often with a fragile mortal tone.

notes.title: Call on the name
notes.body: The chapter closes with the beginning of calling on the name of the Eternal, a turn toward worship or invocation after violence and lineage.
